perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosure . All disclosures of Confidential Information (whether written or oral) by Party One and P a r t y T w o shall (a) remain in confidence for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure , except that any trade secrets or information with respect to Party One products or research and development will remain in confidence in perpetuity; (b) be disclosed only to employees of P a r t y T w o who have executed written confidentiality agreements wit h the employer; and (c) be reproduced or used by P a r t y T w o only to the extent necessary to fulfill it’s obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosure . All disclosures of Confidential Information (whether written or oral) by Party One and P a r t y T w o shall (a) remain in confidence for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure ; (b) be disclosed only to employees of P a r t y T w o ; and (c) be reproduced or used by P a r t y T w o only to the extent necessary to fulfill it’s obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "except that any trade secrets or information with respect to Party One products or research and development will remain in confidence in perpetuity", the contract now contradicts the standard protection afforded to trade secrets under the laws of Ontario and Canada, specifically regarding the duration of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34, s. 36
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian law recognizes the indefinite protection of trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Removing the 'in perpetuity' clause limits this protection, potentially exposing Party One's trade secrets after the three-year period, which contradicts the established legal standard for trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: mpetent jurisdiction, sue for and recover from the person who engaged in the conduct or failed to comply with the order an amount equal to the loss or damage proved to have been suffered by him, together with any additional amount that the court may allow not exceeding the full cost to him of any investigation in connection with the matter and of proceedings under this section. Marginal note: Evidence of prior proceedings (2) Â In any action under subsection (1) against a person, the record of proceedings in any court in which that person was convicted of an offence under Part VI or convicted of or punished for failure to comply with an order of the Tribunal or another court under this Act is, in the absence of any evidence to the contrary, proof that the person against whom the action is brought engaged in conduct that was contrary to a provision of Part VI or failed to comply with an order of the Tribunal or another court under this Act, as the case may be, and any evidence given in those proceedings as to the effect of those acts or omissions on the person bringing the action is evidence thereof in the action. Marginal note: Jurisdiction of Federal Court (3) Â For the purposes of any action under subsection (1), the Federal Court is a court of competent jurisdiction. Marginal note: Limitation (4) Â No action may be brought under subsection (1), (a) Â in the case of an action based on conduct that is contrary to any provision of Part VI, after two years from (i) Â a day on which the conduct was engaged in, or (ii) Â the day on which any criminal proceedings relating thereto were finally disposed of, whichever is the later; and (b) Â in the case of an action based on the failure of any person to comply with an order of the Tribunal or another court, after two years from (i) Â a day on which the order of the Tribunal or court was contravened, or (ii) Â the day on which any criminal proceedings relating thereto were finally disposed of, whichever is the later. perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures perpetual confidentiality for trade secrets and research and development information. The changed contract limits this protection to three years, contradicting the standard expectation for trade secret protection. The law explanation states that Canadian law recognizes indefinite protection for trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Removing the 'in perpetuity' clause undermines this protection. Scraped law snippets don't directly address trade secrets, but the omission of perpetual protection conflicts with the general understanding and legal precedence of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Party One and P a r t y Two shall protect shared Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it normally exercises to protect its own confidential information of similar nature, but at a minimum with a reasonable degree of care to prevent its unauthorized use, dissemination or publication.
perturbation.changed_text: Party One and P a r t y Two shall protect shared Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of care to prevent its unauthorized use, dissemination or publication.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a dual standard of care: the party's usual standard, but at least a reasonable one. The modified text removes the reference to the party's normal practices, reducing the standard of care to only 'reasonable'. This weakens the protection of Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario law and common law principles require a high standard of care for confidential information, especially when a party normally maintains a higher standard for its own information. Removing the reference to the party's typical level of care may fail to meet the legal threshold for adequate protection, potentially leading to liability for breach of confidence.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the standard of care for confidential information by removing the requirement to protect it with the same degree of care used for the party's own confidential information, and only requiring a 'reasonable' degree of care. The Law Explanation suggests this could fail to meet the legal threshold for adequate protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address standards of care for confidential information, the general principle that laws impose minimum standards, supports the assertion that removing a higher, self-imposed standard in favor of a generic 'reasonable' standard creates a legally questionable situation. The Omission of the higher standard weakens the obligation and potentially contradicts legal expectations for protecting sensitive information.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall be entitled to disclose Confidential Information of the other par ty where compelled by law to so disclose pursuant to any legal, judicial or administrative proceeding, provided the disclosing party provides all reasonable prior notice to the other party to allow it to seek a protective order or other appropriate relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall be entitled to disclose Confidential Information of the other party where compelled by law to so disclose pursuant to any legal, judicial or administrative proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "provided the disclosing party provides all reasonable prior notice to the other party to allow it to seek a protective order or other appropriate relief", the contract eliminates the obligation to provide notice before disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion. This weakens the other party's ability to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.law_citation: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), SC 2000, c 5, s 7
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to be transparent about their information handling practices. While it allows for disclosure when legally required, best practices and related legal interpretations suggest a duty to notify the affected party when possible to allow them to take steps to protect their information. Removing the notice provision contradicts the spirit and potential legal expectations under Canadian privacy law.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a provision for providing reasonable prior notice before disclosing confidential information, allowing the other party to seek a protective order. The changed text removes this notice provision, potentially contradicting the spirit of transparency and the affected party's ability to protect their information. The Law Explanation indicates that PIPEDA requires organizations to be transparent and that best practices suggest a duty to notify. Scraped Law Snippet 2 generally describes PIPEDA and its principles, but doesn't explicitly detail a notification requirement. However, the removal of the existing notice provision in the original contract, in light of PIPEDA's broader aim for transparency as described in the Law Explanation, supports the presence of a contradiction. The removal directly undermines the other party's opportunity to mitigate potential harm from the disclosure, thereby contradicting legal expectations under Canadian privacy law.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Ontario and the laws of Canada applicable therein. Each party hereby attorn s to the jurisdiction of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Ontario. Each party hereby attorn s to the jurisdiction of Ontario.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "and the laws of Canada applicable therein", the contract implies that only Ontario provincial law applies, potentially excluding relevant federal laws, particularly those relating to intellectual property or competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Federal Jurisdiction over Intellectual Property
perturbation.law_explanation: The Constitution Act, 1867, grants the federal government exclusive jurisdiction over intellectual property matters, including patents and copyrights. Limiting the governing law solely to Ontario provincial law creates a legal risk, as federal laws relating to IP rights would also apply regardless of the contract's wording. Removing this clause could lead to confusion and disputes over the applicable law.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies adherence to both Ontario law and applicable Canadian federal law. The changed text omits "and the laws of Canada applicable therein," potentially excluding federal laws. The Law Explanation emphasizes the federal government's exclusive jurisdiction over intellectual property under the Constitution Act, 1867, and suggests that limiting the governing law to Ontario law creates a risk, as federal laws relating to IP rights would also apply regardless of the contract's wording. The removal of reference to Canadian federal law could lead to disputes, implying the altered contract could be interpreted as not fully compliant with applicable laws, thereby creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Constitution Act, 1867, which is relevant to the contradiction.
